title: Single-nucleus multimodal spatial transcriptomics reveals spatial colocalization of neoantigen-expressing tumor cells and cognate T cells - Nature Biotechnology
text:
AbstractImproved methods to identify therapeutically relevant tumor neoantigens and their cognate T cells would aid the development of precision medicines for cancer. Here, we developed Slide-GoTags, a droplet-based single-nucleus spatial transcriptomics approach that characterizes neoantigen-specific immunity by integrating targeted transcript genotyping and T cell receptor (TCR) sequencing with single-nucleus RNA sequencing from the same slice of frozen tissue. Application of Slide-GoTags to mouse and human tumors revealed colocalization of clonally expanded, neoantigen-specific T cells with tumor cells expressing their cognate neoantigen. We also identified distinct spatial immune landscapes shaped by anti-PD1 or anti-CTLA4 blockade in mouse colorectal tumors. Across human tumor types, Slide-GoTags detected TCR–neoantigen interactions through spatial proximity and identified an enrichment of interferon-driven immunogenicity niches in immunologically ‘hot’ tumors compared to ‘cold’ tumors. These niches harbored three T cell clonotypes that colocalized with genotyped neoantigens, highlighting a spatially organized antitumor immune response. Collectively, Slide-GoTags establishes a framework for in situ mapping of T cell–tumor interactions directly from individual tissue.

Data availability

Slide-GoTags datasets were deposited to the Broad Institute Single Cell Portal under accession numbers SCP3655 (MC38-SIINFEKL), SCP3657 (MC38 tumors following immune checkpoint blockade—experiment 1), SCP3660 (MC38 tumors following immune checkpoint blockade—experiment 2) and SCP3667 (human tumors). Source data are provided with this paper.
Code availability

Code for processing spatial sequencing libraries is available from GitHub (https://github.com/amitsud/Slide-GoTags).

Sud and J.Y.G. developed the Slide-GoTags protocol and performed the experiments, with help from J.A.W., A.J.C.R., G.J.M., W.S.L., M.V.O., S.L., E.L. and K.J.L. A.N., A. Additional informationPublisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.Extended dataExtended Data Fig. 1 Cell type assignment and gene expression in MC38-SIINFEKL mouse tumor.a, Schematic of the genotyping workflow combining the Nanoranger pipeline21, with Slide-GoTags for variant detection and genotype assignment to single nuclei. b, UMAP of snRNA-seq profiles, colored by cell type annotation. c, Alluvial plot showing clonotype frequencies of TRA and TRB CDR3 sequences detected by 10x Genomics Chromium single-cell V(D)J kit and Slide-GoTags. Slide-GoTags showed higher sensitivity, detecting more unique clonotypes (orange; 6 TRA and 15 TRB) compared to 10x V(D)J kit (2 TRA and 3 TRB). Shared OT-I clones were consistently detected across both platforms. d, Alluvial plot as in panel c, showing clonotype frequencies detected by the 10x Genomics Chromium single-cell V(D)J kit and Slide-GoTags in a human melanoma tumor. e, Distribution of normalized SIINFEKL gene expression across cell types. f, UMAP of tumor cells (light blue), SIINFEKL-genotyped tumor cells (blue), OT-I T cells expressing CDR3 TRA/TRB (red), and other T cells with non-OT-I CDR3 sequences (pink). g, Bar plot showing detection rates of TRA, TRB, and SIINFEKL transcripts across two independent Slide-GoTags replicates generated from separate tissue sections. P-values were calculated using two- sided Fisher’s exact test. h. Spatial mapping of a T cell module score. i, Spatial mapping of SIINFEKL gene expression.Extended Data Fig. 2 Neoantigen genotyping in the MC38 mouse model treated with ICB.a, UMAP of snRNA-seq profiles, colored by cell type annotation across all tumors. b, UMAP of Rpl18 p.Q125R genotyped nuclei: Rpl18-WT (light blue) and Rpl18-MUT (blue), with T cells expressing Rpl18 p.Q125R specific CDR3 TRA/TRB (red) or other TCR clonotypes (pink) across all tumors. c, UMAP of neoantigen-genotyped tumor nuclei from MC38 tumors, combining ICB-treated and untreated control mice, with quantification of WT and MUT nuclei. Individual genotype maps are shown for: Adgpk9 p.R304M; Atg9a p.L299Q; Cpne1 p.L299Q; Tmem135 p.R421L; Dpagt1 p.V213L; Ndfip2 p.A238P. d, Bar plot of T cell frequencies across treatment conditions profiled by Slide-GoTags. Each dot represents one mouse; untreated, n = 1; anti-CTLA-4, n = 3; anti-PD-1, n = 3. Bars indicate mean ± s.d. e, Bar plot of the top 30 most frequent TRA and TRB CDR3 detected by snRNA-seq. Rpl18 p.Q125R specific TCRs are red; other clonotypes are pink. f, Frequency of cell types across the various treatment conditions. g, Fibroblast classified as Cancer-Associated Fibroblasts (CAFs) based on the highest-scoring CAF gene module75 across the anti-PD1 treated mice. Dotes indicate the mean score for each mouse (n = 3). Statistical significance was assessed using one-sided paired t-tests. h, Immunofluorescent staining of tumor sections from untreated, anti-CTLA4 and anti-PD1 treated mice (n = 6 mice per group) antibodies against S100A4 and CD74 (antigen-presenting CAFs;apCAFs)36,37, and CD3 (T cells). Each point represents the total number of T cells colocalized with apCAFs per mouse, summed across n = 36 images per mouse. Boxes indicate median and interquartile range; whiskers denote range. Statistical significance was assessed using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test. i, Representative immunofluorescence image of an anti-PD-1 treated tumor showing nuclei (DAPI, blue), T cells (CD3, red), and dual expression of CD74 (green) and S100A4 (white) marking apCAFs. Staining was performed on n = 6 mice per group, as quantified in h. j, Selected spatially co-occurring receptor-ligand interactions between specific sender-receiver cell type pairs. P values were adjusted for multiple comparisons across tested receptor-ligand pairs using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 3 Slide-GoTags spatial mapping of MC38 neoantigens during ICB.Spatial mapping of tumor nuclei genotyped for Adgpk9 p.R304M (green), Atg9a p.L299Q (pink), Cpne1 p.L299Q (blue), Ndfip2 p.A238P (purple), Tmem135 p.R421L (yellow) and Dpagt1 p.V213L (orange) across anti-PD1 and anti-CTLA4 treated MC38 mouse tumors.Extended Data Fig. 4 Spatial profiling of NSUN5 mutations and cognate TCRs in ccRCC.a, UMAP embedding of snRNA-seq profiles of ccRCC_1 sections A-D, colored by cell type annotation. b, Spatial mapping of sections A-D, colored by cell type (as in a). c, Cell type frequency across RCC_1 sections colored by cell type (as in a). d, UMAP of NSUN5 p.Q18K-genotyped nuclei across all four sections: NSUN5-WT (light blue) and NSUN5-MUT (blue), with T cells expressing NSUN5 p.Q18K-specific CDR3 TRA/TRB (red), tumor-specific CDR3 TRA/TRB (orange), or other clonotype CDR3s (pink). e, Normalized NSUN5 genotyping across cell types (WT, light blue; MUT, blue). f, Spatial mapping of NSUN5 expression in RCC_1_A. g, Alluvial plot of TCR clonotype frequencies across sections A-D. NSUN5-specific TCRs are red, tumor-specific TCRs are orange, and the other TCRs are gray. h-j, Spatial mapping of NSUN5 p.Q18K genotyped nuclei and T cells expressing TCRs, NSUN5-WT (light blue) and NSUN5-MUT (blue), with T cells expressing NSUN5- p.Q18K specific CDR3 TRA/TRB (red), tumor-specific CDR3 TRA/TRB (orange), or other clonotype CDR3s (pink).Extended Data Fig. 5 TCR clonotype distribution, functional states, and platform comparison in ccRCC.a,b, Bar plot of top 30 TRA and TRB CDR3 frequencies in T cells from snRNA-seq across four sections of a ccRCC specimen. NSUN5 p.Q18K-specific TCRs are red; Tumor specific TCRs are orange; other TCR clonotypes are pink. c, Exhaustion module score in T cells grouped by clonotype across sections A-D. Statistical significance was determined using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test comparing NSUN5 TCRs (P = 1.3 × 10−6) or tumor-specific TCRs (P = 9.73 × 10−13) with other clonotypes. d, Proportion of T cells bearing NSUN5 p.Q18K neoantigen-specific TCRs (red), tumor-specific TCRs (orange), or other clonotypes (pink), detected by Slide-GoTags (purple outline; section RCC_1_A) or Slide-TCR-seq (gray outline). Statistical significance was assessed using two-sided Fisher’s exact tests with Benjamini-Hochberg correction for multiple comparisons (Padj, adjusted P value). e,f, Percentage of T cells with detected TCRα (top) or TCRβ (bottom) CDR3 sequences, colored by TCR category as in d. g, Spatial distribution of genotyped tumor cells and TCR-bearing T cells profiled by Slide-TCR-seq.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 6 Spatial analysis of NSUN5-mutant tumor cells and cognate T cells across ccRCC sections.a, Spatial colocalization using NMS between NSUN5-MUT or NSUN5-WT tumor cells and T cells expressing NSUN5 p.Q18K-specific TCRs (red), tumor-specific TCRs (orange), or other TCRs (pink), in sections B-D. b, NMS colocalization of tumor cells grouped by NSUN5 expression level. c, Nearest distance between NSUN5-MUT tumor cells and NSUN5 TCR (red), tumor-specific TCRs (orange), or other TCRs (pink), and correlation with the chemokine/chemokine receptor module score in section A. The P value was calculated using linear regression to assess the significance of the association between NSUN5 p.Q18K-specific T cell distance to NSUN5 p.Q18K tumor cells and the chemokine/chemokine receptor module score. d, KDE analysis of NSUN5 p.Q18K genotyped tumor nuclei and NSUN5 p.Q18K specific TCRs, in section A.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 7 IFN gene set enrichment in ccRCC.a, Proportion of spatially mapped tumor cells genotyped for NSUN5, comparing Region 1 and 2 in section A. b, Exhaustion module score in T cells stratified by spatial region in section A. Statistical significance was determined using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test (P = 0.016). c, Cytotoxicity module score in NSUN5 TCR-expressing T cells stratified by TCR avidity. Statistical significance was determined using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test (P = 0.004). d, Volcano plot of differentially expressed genes between tumor Regions 1 and 2. Statistical significance was determined using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test with Bonferroni correction. e, Gene Ontology enrichment of differentially expressed genes in tumor cells enriched in region 1 section A (from d). P values were adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Benjamini-Hochberg method. f, Heatmap of interferon and MHC II module scores (MP17 and MP18), comparing Regions 1 and 2. g, Heatmap comparing interferon and MHC II module scores across the four ccRCC sections. h, RCC_1 tumor section profiled with Slide-seq showing the spatial distribution of the MP17 interferon and MHC II module score. i-k, KDE plots of gene expression: h, MHC class I genes in tumor cells; i, MHC class II genes in tumor cells; j, IFN-γ expression in T cells. l, Volcano plot of spatial interaction intensity scores for receptor-ligand pairs between regions in tumor cells. P values were adjusted for multiple comparisons across tested receptor-ligand pairs using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 8 Spatially mapped receptor-ligand interaction in RCC_1.Spatial mapping of interaction intensity scores for selected receptor-ligand pairs shown in Fig. 3h.Extended Data Fig. 9 Pathways enriched in immunogenicity niches across tumor types.a, UMAP embedding of snRNA-seq T cell profiles from all Slide-tags samples, colored by cell type annotation. b, Heatmap of spatial overlap between T cell density and MP17-expressing tumor cells, grouped by tumor. Red, high overlap; blue, low overlap. c, Cell type frequency in immunogenicity-rich and poor regions across the tumor types. d, Volcano plot of differentially expressed genes in T cells from immunogenicity-rich vs. poor regions. Statistical significance was determined using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test with Bonferroni correction. e, Gene Ontology analysis of differentially expressed genes (as in d) in T cells from immunogenicity rich regions. P values were adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Benjamini-Hochberg method. f, Distribution of CD8+ gene signature score48 in immune niche-rich versus immune niche-poor regions, calculated using Ucell method74. Statistical significance was determined using two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum tests with Bonferroni correction. g, Composition of neighboring cell types proximal to CD8+ T cell subpopulations within 200 µm radius.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 10 TLS-associated overlap of MP17 tumor cells and T cell dense regions.a, Selected spatially co-occurring receptor-ligand interactions between specific sender-receiver cell type pairs per patient, highlighting interactions involving Progenitor-Exhausted (Progen_Ex) and Terminally Exhausted (Terminal_Ex) CD8+ T cells. P values were adjusted for multiple comparisons across tested receptor-ligand pairs using the Benjamini-Hochberg method. b, Representative immunofluorescent staining of tumor sections for CD3+ T cells (red), CD20+ B cells (green), and DAPI (blue), with TLS-like regions indicated by dashed boxes (top). KDE analysis of T and B cell densities from Slide-GoTags on a consecutive section (bottom). Spatial overlap of high-density T cell, B cell, and MP17 tumor cell regions identifies immune-tumor interaction niches (right). c, Scatter plot showing the percentage of T cell-B cell overlap area versus the percentage of T cell-MP17 tumor cell overlap area. Each point represents the mean across tumors from an individual patient. d, Volcano plot of receptor-ligand interaction intensity scores comparing immunogenicity rich and poor regions. P values were adjusted for multiple comparisons across tested receptor-ligand pairs using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.Source dataSupplementary informationSupplementary Information (download PDF )Supplementary Figs. 1–15 and Note.Reporting Summary (download PDF )Supplementary Tables 1–33 (download XLSX )Excel file containing Supplementary Tables 1–33, including data used for the figures, as well as participant and sample information referenced in the manuscript.Source dataSource Data Figs. 2–5 and Extended Data Figs 2, 5–7, 9 and 10 (download XLSX )Tables for Main and Extended Data figures.Rights and permissionsSpringer Nature or its licensor (e.g. a society or other partner) holds exclusive rights to this article under a publishing agreement with the author(s) or other rightsholder(s); author self-archiving of the accepted manuscript version of this article is solely governed by the terms of such publishing agreement and applicable law.Reprints and permissionsAbout this articleCite this articleNagler, A., Sud, A., Ghannam, J.Y. et al.
